title: Area C Quiz: Ethics, Safety, and Verification (10 questions, pass with 80% or higher)

questions.q: A resident is about to enter the following text into a generative AI: "78-year-old female, Ms. Hanako Yamada (Patient ID: 0012345). Admitted to XX City Hospital on June 2 for pneumonia. Please draft a discharge summary." What is the most significant problem with this input?
choices: That the passage is too long and complex for the AI to produce a summary
That it contains information identifying the patient, such as name, patient ID, admission date, and facility
That using AI to draft a discharge summary is itself banned outright by law
That the honorific attached to the name will make the AI mistake the person for someone real
questions.explanation: This input contains information that directly identifies an individual -- name, patient ID, admission date, and facility name. Because anything entered into a generative AI is sent to the provider's server, this text must not be sent as written. The length of the passage is not the issue (option 1), and using AI to draft a summary is not itself banned by law (option 3). Whether an honorific is attached is not the essential problem (option 4). Once the personal information has been removed or abstracted, requesting a draft remains entirely possible.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why patient information must not be entered into a general-purpose, consumer-facing generative AI service?
choices: Because the AI might use the input to reach out to the patient directly without staff involvement
Because entering the information might cause the hospital's electronic medical record system to shut down by mistake
Because the input reaches the provider's server, may be used for training or quality checks, and cannot be retracted
Because when many users access the service at once, the AI's response speed can temporarily slow down noticeably
questions.explanation: Anything entered into a generative AI is sent over the internet to the provider's server. With general-purpose consumer services, the input may be used for training the model or for quality checks, and there is no way for the user to retract content once it has been sent. This is a serious problem from the standpoint of a healthcare professional's duty of confidentiality and the protection of personal information. The AI reaching out to the patient directly (option 1), the electronic medical record system shutting down (option 2), and a noticeable slowdown in response speed (option 4) are reasons unrelated to protecting what was sent.

questions.q: A medical student wants to use a generative AI to help organize a case they encountered, for use at a study session. What is the most appropriate approach?
choices: Remove name, ID, dates, and facility name, then convert age and dates so the individual cannot be identified
Replacing the name with initials, such as "Mr. Y.H.", is enough; the rest of the information can stay as it is
Entering it from a hospital computer instead of a personal device does not count as sending it externally at all
Entering it late at night, when few other people happen to be using the service, is safe since no record remains
questions.explanation: The appropriate approach is to remove direct identifiers (name, ID, dates, facility name, etc.), then abstract further -- converting age into a decade and dates into relative expressions -- so that the individual cannot be identified, before entering the text. Replacing only the name with initials is not sufficient (option 2), because a combination of a rare disease, age, and region can still identify the individual. Entering data from a hospital computer still counts as sending it to an external server, regardless of which device is used (option 3). The time of day has nothing to do with whether the input is sent or recorded (option 4).

questions.q: A nurse wants to use an illustration created by a generative AI in an in-hospital awareness poster. What is the most important consideration?
choices: Since AI-generated material never raises copyright concerns, it can be used freely without checking
All AI-generated material automatically becomes public domain, so citing the source is enough
Check whether the output resembles an existing work, and also check the terms of service of the tool being used, before using it
As long as it is credited as AI-generated, no legal problem arises even if it resembles an existing work
questions.explanation: The output of a generative AI can sometimes closely resemble an existing work or character contained in its training data, and using it as-is could constitute copyright infringement. In addition, the conditions of use (for example, whether commercial use is allowed) are set out in each service's terms of service, so that also needs checking. The claims that AI-generated material never raises copyright issues (option 1), automatically becomes public domain (option 2), or that crediting it as AI-generated removes any legal problem even if it resembles an existing work (option 4) are all incorrect.

questions.q: A resident wants to enter an entire chapter of a commercially published medical textbook into a generative AI, and print and distribute the resulting summary as a handout for an in-hospital study session. Which of the following best describes this action?
choices: Textbooks are treated as shared property for medical education, so entering and distributing them freely is fine
Once the text has passed through the filter of a generative AI, the rights to the original work simply disappear
There is no legal difference between keeping something as a personal study memo and distributing it as a handout
Distributing the summary could constitute reproduction or adaptation, requiring the rights holder's permission
questions.explanation: A commercially published textbook is a copyrighted work, and printing and distributing a summary created by entering its content could constitute reproduction or adaptation, so obtaining the rights holder's permission may be necessary before distribution. Because the legal treatment can differ between using something solely for one's own study and reproducing/distributing it as a handout, this careful consideration matters specifically when the purpose is distribution. The idea that a textbook is "shared property" and free to use (option 1), that passing it through AI erases the original rights (option 2), and that a personal memo and a distributed handout are treated the same way (option 3) are all incorrect.

questions.q: A generative AI answered, "According to the 2023 guideline of the XX Society, this treatment is considered first-line." What is the most appropriate response?
choices: Always check the guideline itself, confirming that it exists and that its content matches
Because a specific society name and year are given, it can be judged an accurate, real citation
Ask the same AI, "Is that true?" and trust the answer if it says "Yes, it's true"
Ask a different generative AI the same question, and consider the check complete if the same answer comes back
questions.explanation: Generative AI has learned the format and patterns of real documents and citations, so it can present a guideline that does not actually exist, or one that exists but has different content, in a plausible-looking format. Sources must always be checked against the primary source itself, confirming both that it exists and that its content matches. Having a specific society name and year does not guarantee that the citation is real (option 2); asking the same AI to confirm (option 3) or cross-checking with a different AI (option 4) are also not substitutes for checking the primary source.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes bias in generative AI output?
choices: Using the latest generative AI, bias has been completely eliminated as a technical matter
Because generative AI output reflects biases in its training data, it must be handled on the premise that bias is present
Asking a question in Japanese produces an unbiased answer suited to conditions in Japan
Bias is a problem unique to AI that generates images, and does not occur in AI that generates text
questions.explanation: Because generative AI produces output that reflects statistical patterns in its training data, biases present in that data -- involving language, region, gender, era, or values -- also appear in the output. This is an inherent, unavoidable characteristic of the mechanism, so output must be handled on the premise that bias is present. The claim that bias has been completely eliminated as a technical matter (option 1) is incorrect, and asking in Japanese does not resolve biases in the training data (option 3). Bias occurs in both AI that generates text and AI that generates images (option 4).

questions.q: When an image-generation AI was asked several times to produce "an illustration of a doctor and a nurse," the doctor was always drawn as a man and the nurse always as a woman. Which of the following best explains this phenomenon?
choices: The AI looked up the actual staff composition of the user's own institution and drew that exact ratio accordingly
This is simply a neutral output that reflects real-world statistics accurately, without any bias at all
This is just a chance skew occurring this one time, and repeating the request many times will naturally produce a diverse mix
This reflects a bias linking occupation and gender in the training data, correctable by specifying gender in the prompt
questions.explanation: Training data contains many instances of biased associations such as "doctor = male" and "nurse = female," and these statistical patterns are carried straight through into the output; noticing this bias and correcting for it -- for instance, by explicitly specifying gender or diversity in the prompt -- is the user's responsibility. The AI is not looking up the actual staff composition of any institution (option 1), and this cannot be called a neutral output that accurately reflects reality (option 2). Because the bias originates in the underlying data, repeating the request many times will not naturally resolve it (option 3).

questions.q: A pharmacist had a generative AI create patient medication guidance materials. Which combination of considerations is most appropriate for verification before distributing the materials?
choices: Check whether the content matches the package insert, the wording suits the audience, and nothing could cause harm
Check whether the character count matches the specification, whether there are typos, and whether the font reads well
Keep a record of how long generation took, the name of the AI used, and the date the material was generated
Have the AI itself confirm whether the material is accurate, and accept its own reassurance as sufficient proof
questions.explanation: Before using generative AI output in clinical work, verification should be based on three considerations: whether the content matches primary sources such as the package insert or guidelines (fact-checking); whether the wording and content suit the intended readers, such as elderly patients or parents of young children (fit for the audience); and whether there is anything that could cause misunderstanding, anxiety, inappropriate advice, or discriminatory language (harm potential). Checking only the formatting (option 2) or keeping a generation record (option 3) cannot guarantee the safety of the content, and asking the AI itself to confirm (option 4) is not a substitute for verification.

questions.q: During a busy shift, a generative AI's answer came with specific figures and a confident tone, tempting a resident to use it directly in explaining something to a patient. What is the most appropriate judgment?
choices: If the tone sounds confident, accuracy can be judged to be high
If several specific figures are lined up, the content can be regarded as already verified at that point
A confident, fluent tone is not evidence of correctness, so human verification is carried out before use
Verification may be skipped only when the clinician is especially busy
questions.explanation: Generative AI is a mechanism for producing plausible-sounding text, so even incorrect content can be output in a fluent, confident-sounding style. Neither tone nor level of detail is evidence of correctness, and human verification (fact-checking, fit for the audience, and harm potential) is mandatory before using output in clinical work. A confident-sounding tone (option 1) or the presence of specific figures (option 2) cannot be taken as grounds that verification is already complete, and being busy is not an accepted reason to skip verification (option 4).
